programs.name: Golden Buckeye Program
Healthy Aging Grants

eligibility.income_limits: Priority for low-or-moderate income older Ohioans (at or below 300% of Federal Poverty Guidelines or 65% of area median income for the county and household size, based on most recent data). No strict statewide dollar amounts or table; varies by county and household size. Exact limits set locally by counties[1][2][3].
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits specified in program guidelines[1][2][3].
other_requirements: Services targeted at older adults (60+) disproportionately impacted by COVID-19, such as those facing food/housing insecurity, unemployment, or other priority populations in Ohio’s 2023-2026 State Plan on Aging. Counties self-identify at-risk individuals; no direct individual application by families—services accessed via local county-funded providers[1][2][3].

benefits.value: Home and community-based services addressing social determinants of health, including: food assistance (at least 20% of funds in some counties), housing assistance (at least 20% in some counties), internet access/digital literacy (at least 10% in some counties), transportation, nutrition, physical activity, health insurance enrollment, financial stability. Evidence-based/informed programs aligned with Ohio’s 2023-2026 State Plan on Aging. No fixed dollar amounts or hours per individual; $40 million total distributed to all 88 counties (most get minimum $100,000+ based on at-risk older residents)[1][2][3][4][5].

methods: Families do not apply directly to state program; contact local county aging office or funded providers for services. County commissioners applied via Ohio Grants Portal (https://grantsportal.ohio.gov/)—closed 10/13/2023. Project period: 10/1/2023–9/30/2024[2][4].
application.processing_time: N/A for individuals (not direct grant application)[4].
application.waitlist: Varies by county and service; regional variation likely[1][5].
forms: No specific individual forms; counties issue RFIs/applications to local nonprofits (e.g., Montgomery County Healthy Aging Grant Application)[1][3].
documents: No standard list for families; prepare income proof, ID for local services if required by county providers[1][3].

geography.restrictions: Available in all 88 Ohio counties; funds go to county commissioners who subcontract to local 501(c)(3) nonprofits providing services[2][5].
geography.regional_variations: Counties set priorities, providers, fund allocations (e.g., Montgomery County requires 20% housing, 10% internet; Shelby County RFI process). Different wait times, services, offices by county[1][3][5].
offices_or_providers: Local county aging agencies or funded nonprofits (e.g., Montgomery County providers via mcohio.org; check county websites)[1][2].

gotchas: Not a direct grant to families/individuals—counties fund local nonprofits who deliver services. One-time funding (2023-2024); may not renew. Priority on COVID-impacted, not open to all seniors. Families must go through county-specific channels, not state. Application portal closed; now implementation phase[2][4][5].
programs.data_shape_notes: Funds to counties for subcontracting to local providers; no direct individual eligibility/application; county-specific variations in service mix, priorities, and access; time-limited (10/2023-9/2024)[1][2][3][4].

programs.name: Ohio Senior Citizen Tax Credit

income_limits.description: Modified Adjusted Gross Income (AGI) must be less than $100,000
income_limits.note: This is an individual income limit, not household-based. The limit applies to the taxpayer's modified AGI.

other_requirements: You must have been 65 years of age or older during the tax year
You did not previously take the Ohio lump sum distribution credit (these two credits are mutually exclusive — you can only claim one in your lifetime)

benefits.value: $50 fixed credit per tax year; however, a separate Qualifying Retirement Income Credit is also available with a maximum value of $200 for those receiving retirement benefits, annuities, or distributions included in Ohio AGI
benefits.note: The $50 Senior Citizen Credit is a flat amount. The Qualifying Retirement Income Credit (up to $200) is a separate credit for retirement income and has the same $100,000 AGI threshold.

methods: File your current year Ohio state income tax return (Ohio IT 1040)
Phone: 1-800-282-1780 (Ohio Department of Taxation) — for questions about which form to file
application.processing_time: Not specified in available sources; standard tax return processing timelines apply
forms: Ohio Schedule of Credits (to claim the Senior Citizen Credit on line 4)
documents: Proof of age (driver's license, State of Ohio ID card, birth certificate, or passport)
Documentation of modified AGI (typically from federal tax return)
If claiming Qualifying Retirement Income Credit: documentation of retirement benefits, annuities, or distributions (typically Form 1099-R or pension statement)

offices_or_providers: Ohio Department of Taxation (central contact point)

gotchas: This is a tax credit, not a standalone benefit program — you must file an Ohio income tax return to claim it, even if you would otherwise have no tax liability

The $50 Senior Citizen Credit and the Lump Sum Distribution Credit are mutually exclusive. If you claim the Lump Sum Distribution Credit, you permanently forfeit the right to claim the $50 Senior Citizen Credit on any future return
The income limit is $100,000 modified AGI, which is higher than the Homestead Exemption income limit ($38,600 for 2024). Do not confuse these two separate programs

The Qualifying Retirement Income Credit (up to $200) requires that the retirement income be included in your Ohio AGI — some retirement income may be excluded, making you ineligible for this credit even if you receive retirement benefits
You must not have previously claimed the lump sum retirement credit to qualify for either the Senior Citizen Credit or the Qualifying Retirement Income Credit
This credit is nonrefundable, meaning it can only reduce your tax liability to zero; it cannot generate a refund
programs.data_shape_notes: This program has two distinct credits with overlapping eligibility but different benefit amounts and income thresholds. The Senior Citizen Credit is a flat $50, while the Qualifying Retirement Income Credit scales up to $200 based on retirement income. The critical gotcha is the lifetime mutual exclusivity with the Lump Sum Distribution Credit. Unlike the Homestead Exemption (a property tax program), this is purely an income tax credit requiring a filed return. No asset limits, no household size adjustments, and no regional variations.

```json
    "The $50 Senior Citizen Credit and the Lump Sum Distribution Credit are mutually exclusive. If you claim the Lump Sum Distribution Credit, you permanently forfeit the right to claim the $50 Senior Citizen Credit on any future return",

The Senior Citizen Credit is a flat $50, while the Qualifying Retirement Income Credit scales up to $200 based on retirement income. The critical gotcha is the lifetime mutual exclusivity with the Lump Sum Distribution Credit. Unlike the Homestead Exemption (a property tax program), this is purely an income tax credit requiring a filed return. No asset limits, no household size adjustments, and no regional variations."
}
```

## Key Context for Families

**This is not a standalone benefit program** — it's a tax credit claimed on your Ohio state income tax return[7]. Your elderly loved one must file an Ohio IT 1040 to access it, even if they have little or no tax liability[7].

**Two separate credits exist under this umbrella:**

1. **Senior Citizen Credit**: A flat $50 credit for anyone 65+ with modified AGI under $100,000 who didn't previously claim the lump sum distribution credit[1].

2. **Qualifying Retirement Income Credit**: Up to $200 for those receiving retirement benefits, annuities, or distributions that are included in Ohio AGI, with the same $100,000 income threshold[1].

**The permanent trade-off**: If your loved one claims the Lump Sum Distribution Credit (for a one-time lump sum from a pension or retirement plan), they permanently lose eligibility for the $50 Senior Citizen Credit forever[1]. This is a lifetime decision.

**Don't confuse this with the Homestead Exemption**, which is a separate property tax reduction program with a much lower income limit ($38,600 for 2024)[3]. A senior could qualify for both programs if they meet each program's specific requirements.

**To apply**: Call the Ohio Department of Taxation at 1-800-282-1780 if unsure which form to file, then file the Ohio IT 1040 with the Ohio Schedule of Credits[7].

programs.name: Elderly Services Program (ESP)

eligibility.income_limits: No strict income qualification; many services free, others on sliding fee scale based on ability to pay. Eligibility for each service determined by care manager during home visit[4][5][6].
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits mentioned; needs-based assessment only[4][5][6].
other_requirements: Warren County (primarily), Clinton County, Butler County, Hamilton County residents[4][5][6][8]
Require help with Activities of Daily Living (ADLs) and Instrumental Activities of Daily Living (IADLs)[6]
Assessment by ESP care manager during home visit[4][5][6]

benefits.value: Support services to remain safe and independent at home, including care management, home delivered meals (free to eligible seniors), and other coordinated services like personal care or respite as determined by care plan. Specific services vary by individual assessment[4][5][6].

methods: Phone: (513) 695-2271 for Lebanon & surrounding areas (Warren County Community Services Intake)[6]
Online intake form available in some regions (e.g., Community First Solutions ESP Intake Form)[7]
In-person: Home visit assessment follows intake
application.processing_time: Not specified; begins with intake call/email, followed by home visit for assessment[6].
forms: ESP Intake Form (online in some areas)[7]
documents: Not explicitly listed; prepare details on individual needs, ADLs/IADLs, and financial info for sliding scale if applicable[6]

geography.restrictions: Primarily Warren County residents 60+; also serves Clinton, Butler, Hamilton counties (age 60+ in Hamilton, 65+ in Butler/Clinton)[4][5][6][8]
geography.regional_variations: Administered locally: Warren County by Warren County Community Services, Inc. (WCCS) and Council on Aging of Southwestern Ohio; intake/care management varies by area (e.g., Lebanon office). Services funded by county senior services levy[6].
offices_or_providers: Warren County Community Services, Inc. (WCCS), Lebanon & surrounding: (513) 695-2271[6]
Council on Aging of Southwestern Ohio administers[6]

gotchas: Not statewide—limited to specific counties (e.g., Warren primary); not Medicaid/PASSPORT (those have income/asset limits and nursing home-level care needs)[1][2][4]
No automatic eligibility—must have home visit assessment by care manager for each service[4][5][6]
Some services sliding fee, not all free despite 'no income qualification'[6]
Often confused with PASSPORT Medicaid waiver, which requires Medicaid eligibility and higher care needs[1][2]
programs.data_shape_notes: County-specific (Warren-led), no income/asset tests, services determined post-home assessment by care manager, funded by local levy—not statewide Medicaid program

programs.name: Home Repair Program
programs._raw:
I cannot provide a single comprehensive JSON guide to "the Home Repair Program" in Ohio because **Ohio does not have one unified statewide home repair program for elderly homeowners**. Instead, Ohio has a fragmented ecosystem of multiple programs—some statewide, most city or county-specific—each with different eligibility rules, benefit structures, and application processes.

Based on the search results, here's what exists:

## Statewide Programs

**Welcome Home Ohio** (mentioned but not detailed in results) – Income limit up to 120% AMI[1]

**USDA Section 504** – Targets rural areas, seniors 62+, or low-income households; offers $10,000 grant or $40,000 loan[1]

## City/County-Specific Programs

| Program | Location | Age Requirement | Income Limit | Max Benefit | Key Eligibility |
|---------|----------|-----------------|--------------|------------|-----------------|
| Repair-A-Home | Cleveland | None stated | 80% AMI | Not specified | 1-2 family homes; 1+ year ownership |
| Senior Home Repair | Cleveland | 62+ or disabled | 35% AMI | Not specified | 4-step process; requires medical documentation for disability |
| Critical Home Repair | Columbus | None stated | 80% AMI (120% for veterans) | Not specified | 1+ year ownership; current property taxes |
| Critical Repair Program | Cincinnati metro (Hamilton, Warren, Butler, Clermont counties OH; Dearborn, Ohio counties IN; Boone, Kenton, Campbell counties KY) | 65+ OR military-connected | Varies by family size ($5,221–$9,842/month for families of 1–8) | Not specified | Owner-occupied; current on taxes, mortgage, insurance |
| Emergency Home Repair | Montgomery County (outside Dayton/Kettering) | None stated | 80% AMI | Up to $10,000 | Emergency/accessibility repairs |
| Emergency Home Repair & Accessibility | Dayton/Montgomery County | None stated | 50% AMI | Up to $10,000 | Emergency/accessibility repairs |

## Why This Matters for Your Guide

**There is no single application process, no unified income table, and no centralized eligibility determination.** An elderly homeowner in Cleveland qualifies under different rules than one in Columbus or Cincinnati. A 62-year-old in rural Ohio may qualify for USDA Section 504 when urban programs have closed waitlists[1].

## What You Should Do Instead

1. **Identify the specific location** where the elderly person lives (city and county).
2. **Contact the local city/county housing department** or community development office for that jurisdiction.
3. **Use the Ohio Housing Finance Agency's "Power of Home" resource lookup tool**[6] to search over 300 vetted local programs.
4. **For rural areas**, check USDA Section 504 eligibility first[1].

The search results do not provide complete details (phone numbers, specific form names, processing times, or full application procedures) for any single program. To build an accurate guide, you would need to contact each program directly or access their full program guidelines.

programs.name: Franklin County Senior Services Levy Programs (Senior Options)

eligibility.income_limits: Not specified in available sources. Search results do not provide income thresholds or limits.
eligibility.asset_limits: Not specified in available sources. Search results do not provide asset limits or exemptions.
other_requirements: Must reside in Franklin County, Ohio[1]
Younger spouses of eligible residents may participate in meals and other programs[6]

benefits.value: Multiple services including: home delivered meals, homemaker services, personal care, respite care, adult day care, transportation to medical facilities (including wheelchair transportation), emergency response systems, minor home repair, information and referrals, case management, and caregiver relief[1][5]. Adult day care includes health services, personal care, meals, activities, transportation, and may include social work services and rehabilitation therapies[1]. Caregiver Support Program provides assistance on a short-term basis limited to three months[7].
benefits.varies_by: Service availability and intensity vary by individual need and case management assessment. Approximately 70% of enrolled participants receive case management development and ongoing case management, while approximately 30% receive face-to-face case management through partner agencies[3].

methods: Online enrollment (specific URL not provided in sources)[1]
Phone: (614) 525-6200, weekdays 9 AM–4:30 PM ET; Thursday 9 AM–7 PM[1][5]
In-person or mail application methods not explicitly detailed in available sources
forms: Specific form names and numbers not provided in available sources
documents: Not specified in available sources. Residents should contact (614) 525-6200 for required documentation.

geography.regional_variations: No regional variations within Franklin County are documented in available sources. However, the Central Ohio Area Agency on Aging (COAAA) provides all face-to-face assessments and approximately 30% of ongoing case management, suggesting potential coordination across service delivery[3].
offices_or_providers: Franklin County Office on Aging (primary administrator)[3]
Central Ohio Area Agency on Aging (COAAA) — provides assessments and case management[3]
Network of community educational and human service agencies as partners[3]

gotchas: No income or asset limits are documented in available sources — families should verify directly with the program whether means-testing applies, as this is unusual for property-tax-funded senior services[1][2]

The program is entirely dependent on passage of the Senior Services Levy, a property tax renewal. Without levy passage, the program shuts down entirely[2]. The most recent levy cycle (2023–2027) was approved in 2022; families should verify current levy status[4]
Caregiver Support Program assistance is limited to three months, not ongoing[7]
The program serves over 60,000 seniors as of 2023, suggesting potential high demand; processing times and waitlist status are not documented[2]

This is a 'one-stop shop' for information and referrals, but actual service delivery is coordinated through a network of partner agencies — not all services may be directly provided by Franklin County Office on Aging[1][3]
Reasonable donations are suggested for meals and other programs, though eligibility is not contingent on ability to pay[6]
programs.data_shape_notes: Senior Options is a coordinated system of home and community-based care services rather than a single benefit program. Eligibility is based solely on age (60+) and county residency with no documented income or asset testing. Benefits are individualized through case management assessment rather than standardized by tier or household size. The program is entirely funded by a county property tax levy, making it vulnerable to voter approval cycles. Service delivery is distributed across a network of partner agencies, not centralized in a single office.

## Critical Gaps in Available Information

The search results provided do not contain several key details families need:

- **Income and asset limits**: No thresholds are documented. Contact (614) 525-6200 to confirm whether means-testing applies[1].
- **Processing timelines**: No information on how long enrollment takes or whether waitlists exist.
- **Required documentation**: Not specified in sources.
- **Online application URL**: The sources reference online enrollment but do not provide the actual web address.
- **Specific service quantities**: While services are listed, dollar amounts per service, hours per week, or visit frequency are not detailed.

**Recommendation**: Families should call (614) 525-6200 directly to obtain complete eligibility criteria, required documents, and current service availability. The program's "one-stop shop" model is designed to provide personalized guidance[1][5].
